([1935–36](/wiki/1936_Stanley_Cup_Final "1936 Stanley Cup Final"), [1936–37](/wiki/1937_Stanley_Cup_Final "1937 Stanley Cup Final"), [1942–43](/wiki/1943_Stanley_Cup_Final "1943 Stanley Cup Final"), [1949–50](/wiki/1950_Stanley_Cup_Final "1950 Stanley Cup Final"), [1951–52](/wiki/1952_Stanley_Cup_Final "1952 Stanley Cup Final"), [1953–54](/wiki/1954_Stanley_Cup_Final "1954 Stanley Cup Final"), [1954–55](/wiki/1955_Stanley_Cup_Final "1955 Stanley Cup Final"), [1996–97](/wiki/1997_Stanley_Cup_Final "1997 Stanley Cup Final"), [1997–98](/wiki/1998_Stanley_Cup_Final "1998 Stanley Cup Final"), [2001–02](/wiki/2002_Stanley_Cup_Final "2002 Stanley Cup Final"), [2007–08](/wiki/2008_Stanley_Cup_Final "2008 Stanley Cup Final")) |

(colloquially referred to as the **Wings**)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) are a professional [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") team based in [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"). The Red Wings compete in the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL) as a member of the [Atlantic Division](/wiki/Atlantic_Division_(NHL) "Atlantic Division (NHL)") in the [Eastern Conference](/wiki/Eastern_Conference_(NHL) "Eastern Conference (NHL)").[[6]](#cite_note-6) The franchise is one of the so called [Original Six](/wiki/Original_Six "Original Six") teams of the league.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Founded in [1926](/wiki/1926%E2%80%9327_NHL_season "1926–27 NHL season"), the team was known as the **Detroit Cougars** until [1930](/wiki/1929%E2%80%9330_NHL_season "1929–30 NHL season"). For the next two seasons, the team was named the **Detroit Falcons**, before changing their name to the Red Wings in [1932](/wiki/1932%E2%80%9333_NHL_season "1932–33 NHL season").[[8]](#cite_note-Guide-8)

The **Detroit Red Wings** (colloquially referred to as the **Wings**)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) are a professional [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") team based in [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"). The Red Wings compete in the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL) as a member of the [Atlantic Division](/wiki/Atlantic_Division_(NHL) "Atlantic Division (NHL)") in the [Eastern Conference](/wiki/Eastern_Conference_(NHL) "Eastern Conference (NHL)").[[6]](#cite_note-6) The franchise is one of the so called [Original Six](/wiki/Original_Six "Original Six") teams of the league.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Founded in [1926](/wiki/1926%E2%80%9327_NHL_season "1926–27 NHL season"), the team was known as the **Detroit Cougars** until [1930](/wiki/1929%E2%80%9330_NHL_season "1929–30 NHL season"). For the next two seasons, the team was named the **Detroit Falcons**, before changing their name to the Red Wings in [1932](/wiki/1932%E2%80%9333_NHL_season "1932–33 NHL season").[[8]](#cite_note-Guide-8)

The Red Wings have won the most [Stanley Cup](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup") championships of any NHL franchise based in the United States (11),[[9]](#cite_note-9) and are third overall amongst active teams in total Stanley Cup championships, behind the [Montreal Canadiens](/wiki/Montreal_Canadiens "Montreal Canadiens") (24) and [Toronto Maple Leafs](/wiki/Toronto_Maple_Leafs "Toronto Maple Leafs") (13). The Wings played their home games at [Joe Louis Arena](/wiki/Joe_Louis_Arena "Joe Louis Arena") from 1979 until 2017, after playing for 52 years at [Olympia Stadium](/wiki/Olympia_Stadium "Olympia Stadium"). They moved into [Little Caesars Arena](/wiki/Little_Caesars_Arena "Little Caesars Arena") beginning with the 2017–18 season. The Red Wings are one of the most popular and successful franchises in the NHL; fans and sports commentators refer to the Detroit area as "[Hockeytown](/wiki/Hockeytown "Hockeytown")", which has been a registered trademark owned by the franchise since 1996.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Between the [1931–32](/wiki/1931%E2%80%9332_NHL_season "1931–32 NHL season") and [1965–66](/wiki/1965%E2%80%9366_NHL_season "1965–66 NHL season") seasons, the Red Wings missed the playoffs only four times. They struggled between the 1966–67 and 1982–83 seasons, only making the playoffs twice in that stretch. After that, however, from 1983–84 to 2015–16, they made the playoffs 30 times in 32 seasons, including 25 straight from 1990–91 to 2015–16 (not including the canceled [2004–05 season](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_NHL_season "2004–05 NHL season")); in [2006](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_NHL_season "2005–06 NHL season"), this became the longest active [streak of postseason appearances](/wiki/List_of_NHL_franchise_post-season_appearance_streaks "List of NHL franchise post-season appearance streaks") in all of North American professional sports and finished tied for the third-longest streak in NHL history. Since 1983–84, the Red Wings have tallied six [regular season first-place finishes](/wiki/Presidents%27_Trophy "Presidents' Trophy") and have won the Stanley Cup four times ([1997](/wiki/1997_Stanley_Cup_Final "1997 Stanley Cup Final"), [1998](/wiki/1998_Stanley_Cup_Final "1998 Stanley Cup Final"), [2002](/wiki/2002_Stanley_Cup_Final "2002 Stanley Cup Final"), and [2008](/wiki/2008_Stanley_Cup_Final "2008 Stanley Cup Final")).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Red_Wings&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [History of the Detroit Red Wings](/wiki/History_of_the_Detroit_Red_Wings "History of the Detroit Red Wings")

### Early years (1926–1949)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Red_Wings&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early years (1926–1949)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/1926_27_Detroit_Cougars.png/250px-1926_27_Detroit_Cougars.png)](/wiki/File:1926_27_Detroit_Cougars.png)

Team photo from Detroit's inaugural season ([1926–27](/wiki/1926%E2%80%9327_NHL_season "1926–27 NHL season")). The franchise was known as the Detroit Cougars from 1926 to 1930.

Following the [1926 Stanley Cup playoffs](/wiki/1926_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "1926 Stanley Cup playoffs"), during which the [Western Hockey League](/wiki/Western_Canada_Hockey_League "Western Canada Hockey League") (WHL) was widely reported to be on the verge of folding,[[11]](#cite_note-11) the NHL held a meeting on April 17 to consider applications for expansion franchises, at which it was reported that five different groups sought a team for Detroit.[[12]](#cite_note-12) During a subsequent meeting on May 15, 1926,[[13]](#cite_note-13) the NHL owners voted, 6 to 2, to approve a franchise to the Townsend-Seyburn group of Detroit and named [Charles A. Hughes](/wiki/Charles_A._Hughes "Charles A. Hughes") as governor.[[14]](#cite_note-14) WHL owners [Frank](/wiki/Frank_Patrick_(ice_hockey) "Frank Patrick (ice hockey)") and [Lester Patrick](/wiki/Lester_Patrick "Lester Patrick") made a deal to sell the league's players to the NHL and cease league operations. The new Detroit franchise purchased the players of the WHL's [Victoria Cougars](/wiki/Victoria_Cougars "Victoria Cougars"), who had won the Stanley Cup in [1925](/wiki/1925_Stanley_Cup_Final "1925 Stanley Cup Final") and had made the [1926 Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/1926_Stanley_Cup_Final "1926 Stanley Cup Final"), to play for the team. The new Detroit franchise also adopted the Cougars' nickname in honor of the folded franchise.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Since no arena in Detroit was ready at the time, the Cougars played their [first season](/wiki/1926%E2%80%9327_Detroit_Cougars_season "1926–27 Detroit Cougars season") at the [Border Cities Arena](/wiki/Border_Cities_Arena "Border Cities Arena") in [Windsor, Ontario](/wiki/Windsor,_Ontario "Windsor, Ontario").[[16]](#cite_note-1920s_Cougars-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) For the [1927–28 season](/wiki/1927%E2%80%9328_NHL_season "1927–28 NHL season"), the Cougars moved into the new [Detroit Olympia](/wiki/Detroit_Olympia "Detroit Olympia"), which would be their home rink until December 15, 1979.[[18]](#cite_note-Olympia-18) This was also the first season behind the bench for [Jack Adams](/wiki/Jack_Adams "Jack Adams"), who would be the face of the franchise for the next 36 years as either coach or general manager.[[19]](#cite_note-Adams-19)

The Cougars made the Stanley Cup playoffs for the first time in [1929](/wiki/1928%E2%80%9329_NHL_season "1928–29 NHL season"), with [Carson Cooper](/wiki/Carson_Cooper "Carson Cooper") leading the team in scoring.[[16]](#cite_note-1920s_Cougars-16)[[20]](#cite_note-20) The Cougars were outscored 7–2 in the two-game series with the [Toronto Maple Leafs](/wiki/Toronto_Maple_Leafs "Toronto Maple Leafs").[[21]](#cite_note-1929_Cougars-21) In 1930, the Cougars were renamed the Falcons, but their woes continued, as they usually finished near the bottom of the standings, even though they made the playoffs again in [1932](/wiki/1931%E2%80%9332_NHL_season "1931–32 NHL season").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In 1932, the NHL let [grain merchant](/wiki/Grain_trade "Grain trade") [James E. Norris](/wiki/James_E._Norris "James E. Norris"), who had made two previous unsuccessful bids to buy an NHL team, purchase the Falcons. Norris' first act was to rename the team; earlier in the century, Norris had been a member of the [Montreal Amateur Athletic Association](/wiki/Montreal_Amateur_Athletic_Association "Montreal Amateur Athletic Association") (MAAA), a multi-sport club whose winged-wheel emblem derived from its cycling roots, and [whose hockey team](/wiki/Montreal_Hockey_Club "Montreal Hockey Club") won the first Stanley Cup in 1893. Norris decided that a red version of the MAAA "[Winged Wheelers](/wiki/Montreal_AAA_Winged_Wheelers "Montreal AAA Winged Wheelers")" logo would be perfect for a team playing in the "Motor City", and on October 5, 1932, the club was renamed the Detroit Red Wings.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Norris also placed coach Jack Adams on a one-year probation for the [1932–33 NHL season](/wiki/1932%E2%80%9333_NHL_season "1932–33 NHL season").[[24]](#cite_note-24) Adams managed to pass his probationary period by leading the Red Wings to their first-ever playoff series victory, over the [Montreal Maroons](/wiki/Montreal_Maroons "Montreal Maroons").[[25]](#cite_note-25) The team then lost in the semifinals to the [New York Rangers](/wiki/New_York_Rangers "New York Rangers").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In [1934](/wiki/1933%E2%80%9334_NHL_season "1933–34 NHL season"), the Red Wings made the [Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/1934_Stanley_Cup_Final "1934 Stanley Cup Final") for the first time, with [John Sorrell](/wiki/John_Sorrell_(ice_hockey) "John Sorrell (ice hockey)") scoring 21 goals over 47 games and [Larry Aurie](/wiki/Larry_Aurie "Larry Aurie") leading the team in scoring.[[27]](#cite_note-27) However, the [Chicago Black Hawks](/wiki/Chicago_Blackhawks "Chicago Blackhawks") defeated the Red Wings in the Stanley Cup Final, winning the best-of-five series in four games to claim their first title.[[28]](#cite_note-1930s_champs-28) Two seasons later, the Red Wings won their first Stanley Cup in [1936](/wiki/1935%E2%80%9336_NHL_season "1935–36 NHL season"), defeating Toronto in four games.[[28]](#cite_note-1930s_champs-28) Detroit repeated as Stanley Cup champions in [1937](/wiki/1936%E2%80%9337_NHL_season "1936–37 NHL season"), winning over the Rangers in the full five games.[[28]](#cite_note-1930s_champs-28) In 1938, the [Montreal Canadiens](/wiki/Montreal_Canadiens "Montreal Canadiens") and the Red Wings became the first NHL teams to play in Europe, playing in Paris and [London](/wiki/London "London"). The Wings played nine games against the Canadiens and went 3–5–1. They did not play in Europe again until the preseason and start of the [2009–10 NHL season](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_NHL_season "2009–10 NHL season"), in Sweden, against the [St. Louis Blues](/wiki/St._Louis_Blues "St. Louis Blues").[[29]](#cite_note-29)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Gordie_Howe_1946-47.jpg/250px-Gordie_Howe_1946-47.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gordie_Howe_1946-47.jpg)

Making his NHL debut in 1946, [Gordie Howe](/wiki/Gordie_Howe "Gordie Howe") played alongside [Sid Abel](/wiki/Sid_Abel "Sid Abel") and [Ted Lindsay](/wiki/Ted_Lindsay "Ted Lindsay") from 1947 to 1951, forming the [Production Line](/wiki/Production_Line_(ice_hockey) "Production Line (ice hockey)").

The Red Wings made the Stanley Cup Final in three consecutive years during the early 1940s.[[30]](#cite_note-1940s_Wings-30) In [1941](/wiki/1940%E2%80%9341_NHL_season "1940–41 NHL season"), they were swept by the [Boston Bruins](/wiki/Boston_Bruins "Boston Bruins"), and in [1942](/wiki/1941%E2%80%9342_NHL_season "1941–42 NHL season"), they lost a seven-game series to Toronto after winning the first three games.[[30]](#cite_note-1940s_Wings-30) However, in [1943](/wiki/1942%E2%80%9343_NHL_season "1942–43 NHL season"), with [Mud Bruneteau](/wiki/Mud_Bruneteau "Mud Bruneteau") and [Syd Howe](/wiki/Syd_Howe "Syd Howe") scoring 23 and 20 goals, respectively, Detroit won their third Stanley Cup by sweeping the Bruins.[[30]](#cite_note-1940s_Wings-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) Through the rest of the decade, the team made the playoffs every year, and reached the Stanley Cup Final three more times.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-1948_Wings-33)[[34]](#cite_note-1949_Wings-34)

In [1946](/wiki/1946%E2%80%9347_NHL_season "1946–47 NHL season"), one of the greatest players in hockey history came into the NHL with the Red Wings. [Gordie Howe](/wiki/Gordie_Howe "Gordie Howe"), a right winger from [Floral, Saskatchewan](/wiki/Floral,_Saskatchewan "Floral, Saskatchewan"), only scored seven goals and 15 assists in his first season, and would not reach his prime for a few more years.[[35]](#cite_note-35) It was also the last season as head coach for Adams, who stepped down after the season to concentrate on his duties as general manager and was succeeded by minor league coach [Tommy Ivan](/wiki/Tommy_Ivan "Tommy Ivan").[[36]](#cite_note-Production_Line-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) By his second season, Howe was paired with [Sid Abel](/wiki/Sid_Abel "Sid Abel") and [Ted Lindsay](/wiki/Ted_Lindsay "Ted Lindsay") to form what would become one of the great lines in NHL history: the "[Production Line](/wiki/Production_Line_(ice_hockey) "Production Line (ice hockey)")".[[36]](#cite_note-Production_Line-36) Lindsay's 33 goals propelled the Red Wings to the [1948 Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/1948_Stanley_Cup_Final "1948 Stanley Cup Final"), where they were swept by the Maple Leafs.[[33]](#cite_note-1948_Wings-33) Detroit reached the [Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/1949_Stanley_Cup_Final "1949 Stanley Cup Final") again the following season, only to be swept again by Toronto.[[34]](#cite_note-1949_Wings-34)

### Gordie Howe era (1950–1966)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Red_Wings&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Gordie Howe era (1950–1966)")]

During the [1950 semifinals](/wiki/1949%E2%80%9350_NHL_season "1949–50 NHL season"), [Leo Reise Jr.](/wiki/Leo_Reise_Jr. "Leo Reise Jr.") scored the winning goal in overtime, which prevented the Maple Leafs from winning four straight championships.[[38]](#cite_note-1950_Wings-38) In the [1950 Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/1950_Stanley_Cup_Final "1950 Stanley Cup Final"), the Red Wings defeated the New York Rangers in seven games. In game 7, [Pete Babando](/wiki/Pete_Babando "Pete Babando") scored the game winner in double overtime.[[38]](#cite_note-1950_Wings-38) After the game, Lindsay skated around the Olympia ice with the Stanley Cup.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

After being upset by the Montreal Canadiens in the [1951 semifinals](/wiki/1950%E2%80%9351_NHL_season "1950–51 NHL season"), Detroit won its fifth Stanley Cup in [1952](/wiki/1952_Stanley_Cup_Final "1952 Stanley Cup Final"), sweeping both the Maple Leafs and the Canadiens, with the Production Line of Howe, Abel and Lindsay joined by second-year goaltender [Terry Sawchuk](/wiki/Terry_Sawchuk "Terry Sawchuk").[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-1952_Wings-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) Detroit became the first team in 17 years to go undefeated in the playoffs.[[41]](#cite_note-1952_Wings-41)[[43]](#cite_note-43) They also scored 24 playoff goals, compared to Toronto and Montreal's combined total of 5.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Abel left the Red Wings for Chicago during the off-season, and his spot on the roster was replaced by [Alex Delvecchio](/wiki/Alex_Delvecchio "Alex Delvecchio").[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46) In December 1952, James E. Norris died.[[47]](#cite_note-Maguerite-47) He was succeeded as team president by his daughter, [Marguerite](/wiki/Marguerite_Norris "Marguerite Norris"), which made her the first woman to head an NHL franchise.[[47]](#cite_note-Maguerite-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/1952_Detroit_Red_Wings.jpg/250px-1952_Detroit_Red_Wings.jpg)](/wiki/File:1952_Detroit_Red_Wings.jpg)

Team photo of the 1951–52 Detroit Red Wings. They won the [Stanley Cup](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup") that season.

Following another playoff upset in [1953](/wiki/1952%E2%80%9353_NHL_season "1952–53 NHL season") at the hands of the Bruins,[[49]](#cite_note-49) the Red Wings won back-to-back Stanley Cups, beating the rival powerhouse Montreal Canadiens. Both of the Stanley Cup Final series played between the two teams were decided in seven games. The seventh game during the [1954 Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/1953%E2%80%9354_NHL_season "1953–54 NHL season") was won with one of the oddest cup winning goals ever, when the 5'7" left winger [Tony Leswick](/wiki/Tony_Leswick "Tony Leswick"), known more for his relentless checking than scoring prowess, shot a puck towards the Montreal goal from the middle of the ice. Habs defenseman [Doug Harvey](/wiki/Doug_Harvey_(ice_hockey) "Doug Harvey (ice hockey)") tried to gain control of the wobbly puck with his glove but instead redirected it past Montreal goalie [Gerry McNeil](/wiki/Gerry_McNeil "Gerry McNeil").[[50]](#cite_note-50) The repeat of the series [the season after](/wiki/1954%E2%80%9355_NHL_season "1954–55 NHL season") was closely contested, as all seven games were won by the home team, with Detroit taking the seventh game. Montreal was sorely lacking its all-star [Maurice Richard](/wiki/Maurice_Richard "Maurice Richard"), who was suspended after hitting a linesman during the regular season, and the Red Wings' stars carried their team, as Lindsay scored four goals in a single game and Howe scored 20 points during the playoffs, 12 of which during the Stanley Cup Final series, all new records in the league.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

The 1954–55 season ended a run of seven straight regular season titles, an NHL record.[[52]](#cite_note-52) During the 1955 off-season, Marguerite Norris lost an intra-family power struggle, and was forced to turn over the Red Wings to her younger brother [Bruce](/wiki/Bruce_Norris_(ice_hockey) "Bruce Norris (ice hockey)").[[53]](#cite_note-53) Detroit and Montreal once again met, in the [1956 Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/1955%E2%80%9356_NHL_season "1955–56 NHL season"), but this time the Canadiens won the Stanley Cup, their first of five in a row.[[54]](#cite_note-54) In [1957](/wiki/1956%E2%80%9357_NHL_season "1956–57 NHL season"), Lindsay, who had scored 30 goals and led the league in assists with 55, teamed up with Harvey to help start the [National Hockey League Players' Association](/wiki/National_Hockey_League_Players%27_Association "National Hockey League Players' Association") (NHLPA).[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) As a result, he and [goaltender](/wiki/Goaltender "Goaltender") [Glenn Hall](/wiki/Glenn_Hall "Glenn Hall") were promptly traded to Chicago.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

In [1958–59](/wiki/1958%E2%80%9359_NHL_season "1958–59 NHL season"), the Red Wings missed the playoffs for the first time in 21 years.[[58]](#cite_note-58) However, within a couple of years, the franchise was able to rejuvenate itself. The Red Wings made the Stanley Cup Final four times between [1960–61](/wiki/1960%E2%80%9361_NHL_season "1960–61 NHL season") and [1965–66](/wiki/1965%E2%80%9366_NHL_season "1965–66 NHL season") seasons.[[59]](#cite_note-1960s_Wings-59) However, they came away empty-handed.[[59]](#cite_note-1960s_Wings-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### "Dead Wings" era (1967–1982)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Red_Wings&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: \"Dead Wings\" era (1967–1982)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Detroit_December_2015_59_%28Joe_Louis_Arena%29.jpg/250px-Detroit_December_2015_59_%28Joe_Louis_Arena%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Detroit_December_2015_59_(Joe_Louis_Arena).jpg)

On December 27, 1979, the Red Wings played their first game at [Joe Louis Arena](/wiki/Joe_Louis_Arena "Joe Louis Arena"), moving from the [Detroit Olympia](/wiki/Detroit_Olympia "Detroit Olympia").

Only a year after making the [1966 Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/1966_Stanley_Cup_Final "1966 Stanley Cup Final"), the Red Wings finished a distant fifth, 24 points out of the playoffs.[[61]](#cite_note-61) It was the beginning of a slump that they would not emerge from in almost 20 years. This period is derisively known as the "Dead Wings" era.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

One factor in the Red Wings' decline was the end of the old development system.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Another factor was [Ned Harkness](/wiki/Ned_Harkness "Ned Harkness"), who was hired as coach in [1970](/wiki/1970%E2%80%9371_NHL_season "1970–71 NHL season") and was promoted to general manager midway through the season. A successful college hockey coach, Harkness tried to force his two-way style of play on a veteran Red Wings team resistant to change. They chafed under his rule in which he demanded short hair and no smoking, and put other rules in place regarding drinking and phone calls.[[64]](#cite_note-brokenwings-64) Harkness was forced to resign in 1974, ending the period colloquially referred to as "Darkness with Harkness".[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-Howe-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67)

During the expansion season of [1967–68](/wiki/1967%E2%80%9368_NHL_season "1967–68 NHL season"), the Red Wings acquired longtime star left-winger [Frank Mahovlich](/wiki/Frank_Mahovlich "Frank Mahovlich") from the defending Cup champions in Toronto. Mahovlich would go on a line with Howe and Delvecchio, and in [1968–69](/wiki/1968%E2%80%9369_NHL_season "1968–69 NHL season"), he scored a career-high 49 goals and had two All-Star seasons in Detroit.[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70) However, Mahovlich was traded to Montreal in [1971](/wiki/1970%E2%80%9371_NHL_season "1970–71 NHL season"), while Howe announced his retirement the same year.[[66]](#cite_note-Howe-66)[[71]](#cite_note-Trades-71) Throughout the decade, the Red Wings were hampered due to a number of factors.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

On December 27, 1979, during the [1979–80 season](/wiki/1979%E2%80%9380_NHL_season "1979–80 NHL season"), the Red Wings officially began playing at the [Joe Louis Arena](/wiki/Joe_Louis_Arena "Joe Louis Arena") after leaving the Olympia, where they had played since 1927.[[73]](#cite_note-73) In 1982, after 50 years of family ownership, Bruce Norris sold the Red Wings to [Mike Ilitch](/wiki/Mike_Ilitch "Mike Ilitch"), founder of the pizza chain [Little Caesars](/wiki/Little_Caesars "Little Caesars").[[74]](#cite_note-74)

### Steve Yzerman era (1983–2006)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Red_Wings&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Steve Yzerman era (1983–2006)")]

In [1983](/wiki/1983_NHL_entry_draft "1983 NHL entry draft"), the Red Wings drafted [Steve Yzerman](/wiki/Steve_Yzerman "Steve Yzerman"), a center from the [Peterborough Petes](/wiki/Peterborough_Petes "Peterborough Petes"), with their first-round pick. He led the team in scoring in [his rookie year](/wiki/1983%E2%80%9384_NHL_season "1983–84 NHL season").[[75]](#cite_note-75) That season, with [John Ogrodnick](/wiki/John_Ogrodnick "John Ogrodnick"), [Ivan Boldirev](/wiki/Ivan_Boldirev "Ivan Boldirev"), [Ron Duguay](/wiki/Ron_Duguay "Ron Duguay"), and [Brad Park](/wiki/Brad_Park "Brad Park"), Detroit made the playoffs for the first time in six years,[[76]](#cite_note-76) with Park ended up winning the [Bill Masterton Memorial Trophy](/wiki/Bill_Masterton_Memorial_Trophy "Bill Masterton Memorial Trophy").[[77]](#cite_note-77) He was later asked to coach the Red Wings after they fired [Harry Neale](/wiki/Harry_Neale "Harry Neale") 35 games into the [1985–86 season](/wiki/1985%E2%80%9386_NHL_season "1985–86 NHL season"),[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80) however, he was fired on June 3, 1986, after they finished last place with a 17–57–6 record for only 40 points, the worst record in the league.[[81]](#cite_note-81) This was the same year that the Red Wings added [enforcer](/wiki/Enforcer_(ice_hockey) "Enforcer (ice hockey)") [Bob Probert](/wiki/Bob_Probert "Bob Probert"), one of the most familiar faces of the team during the 1980s and 1990s.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Steve_Yzerman_%28Columbus_OH%2C_2005%29.jpg/250px-Steve_Yzerman_%28Columbus_OH%2C_2005%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Steve_Yzerman_(Columbus_OH,_2005).jpg)

Named team captain in 1986, [Steve Yzerman](/wiki/Steve_Yzerman "Steve Yzerman") captained the Red Wings until his retirement in 2006.

In the [1986–87 season](/wiki/1986%E2%80%9387_NHL_season "1986–87 NHL season"), with Yzerman, now the captain following the departure of [Danny Gare](/wiki/Danny_Gare "Danny Gare"), joined by [Petr Klima](/wiki/Petr_Klima "Petr Klima"), [Adam Oates](/wiki/Adam_Oates "Adam Oates"), [Gerard Gallant](/wiki/Gerard_Gallant "Gerard Gallant"), defenseman [Darren Veitch](/wiki/Darren_Veitch "Darren Veitch"), and new head coach [Jacques Demers](/wiki/Jacques_Demers "Jacques Demers"), the Red Wings won a playoff series for only the second time in the modern era.[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-1987_Wings-84) They made it all the way to the conference finals against the powerful [Edmonton Oilers](/wiki/Edmonton_Oilers "Edmonton Oilers"), but lost to the eventual Stanley Cup champions in five games.[[84]](#cite_note-1987_Wings-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85) In [1988](/wiki/1987%E2%80%9388_NHL_season "1987–88 NHL season"), they won their first division title in 23 years. They did so, however, in a relatively weak division, as no other team in the Norris finished above .500.[[86]](#cite_note-86) As was the case in the previous season, they made it to the conference finals only to lose again to the eventual Stanley Cup champion Oilers in five games.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

In [1989](/wiki/1988%E2%80%9389_NHL_season "1988–89 NHL season"), Yzerman scored a career-best 65 goals, but Detroit was upset in the first round by the Chicago Blackhawks.[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90) The following season, Yzerman scored 62 goals, but the team missed the playoffs.[[91]](#cite_note-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92)

After the season, Demers was fired and was replaced by [Bryan Murray](/wiki/Bryan_Murray_(ice_hockey) "Bryan Murray (ice hockey)") as the new head coach.[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-Murray-94) Murray was unable to get them back over .500, but they returned to the playoffs.[[94]](#cite_note-Murray-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95) Yzerman was joined by [Sergei Fedorov](/wiki/Sergei_Fedorov "Sergei Fedorov"), who would be an award-winner and frequent all-star for the team during the 1990s.[[96]](#cite_note-96) In 1991, the team signed free agent [Ray Sheppard](/wiki/Ray_Sheppard "Ray Sheppard"), who would score a career-best 52 goals three years later.[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98) In 1993, the Red Wings acquired top defenseman [Paul Coffey](/wiki/Paul_Coffey "Paul Coffey").[[99]](#cite_note-99) Also joining the Red Wings around this time were draft picks [Vladimir Konstantinov](/wiki/Vladimir_Konstantinov "Vladimir Konstantinov"), [Nicklas Lidstrom](/wiki/Nicklas_Lidstr%C3%B6m "Nicklas Lidström"), [Vyacheslav Kozlov](/wiki/Vyacheslav_Kozlov "Vyacheslav Kozlov"), [Darren McCarty](/wiki/Darren_McCarty "Darren McCarty"), and [Chris Osgood](/wiki/Chris_Osgood "Chris Osgood").[[100]](#cite_note-Konstantinov-100)[[101]](#cite_note-101)

#### The Russian Five and back-to-back Stanley Cups (1994–1998)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Red_Wings&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: The Russian Five and back-to-back Stanley Cups (1994–1998)")]

In [1993](/wiki/1993%E2%80%9394_NHL_season "1993–94 NHL season"), former Montreal Canadiens coach [Scotty Bowman](/wiki/Scotty_Bowman "Scotty Bowman") was hired as the new head coach.[[102]](#cite_note-102) In his second season, the [lockout](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_NHL_lockout "1994–95 NHL lockout")-shortened [1994–95 NHL season](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_NHL_season "1994–95 NHL season"), Bowman guided Detroit to its first [Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/1995_Stanley_Cup_Final "1995 Stanley Cup Final") appearance in 29 years, only to be swept by the [New Jersey Devils](/wiki/New_Jersey_Devils "New Jersey Devils").[[103]](#cite_note-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105)

During the [1995–96 season](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_NHL_season "1995–96 NHL season"), the Red Wings won a then NHL record 62 games. However, after defeating the St. Louis Blues in seven games, they would fall in the conference finals to the eventual Stanley Cup champions, the [Colorado Avalanche](/wiki/Colorado_Avalanche "Colorado Avalanche").[[106]](#cite_note-1996_Wings-106)[[107]](#cite_note-107)[[108]](#cite_note-108)

The [following season](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_NHL_season "1996–97 NHL season"), the Red Wings acquired [Brendan Shanahan](/wiki/Brendan_Shanahan "Brendan Shanahan") and [Larry Murphy](/wiki/Larry_Murphy_(ice_hockey) "Larry Murphy (ice hockey)").[[109]](#cite_note-109)[[110]](#cite_note-110) In the playoffs, they would defeat the St. Louis Blues, the [Mighty Ducks of Anaheim](/wiki/Anaheim_Ducks "Anaheim Ducks") and the Avalanche in the first three rounds. In the [1997 Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/1997_Stanley_Cup_Final "1997 Stanley Cup Final"), the Red Wings swept the [Philadelphia Flyers](/wiki/Philadelphia_Flyers "Philadelphia Flyers"). It was their first Stanley Cup since [1955](/wiki/1955_Stanley_Cup_Final "1955 Stanley Cup Final"), breaking the longest drought (42 years long) in the league at that time. [Mike Vernon](/wiki/Mike_Vernon_(ice_hockey) "Mike Vernon (ice hockey)") was awarded the [Conn Smythe Trophy](/wiki/Conn_Smythe_Trophy "Conn Smythe Trophy").[[111]](#cite_note-111)

Misfortune befell the Red Wings six days after their championship; defenseman [Vladimir Konstantinov](/wiki/Vladimir_Konstantinov "Vladimir Konstantinov"), one of the members of the "[Russian Five](/wiki/Russian_Five "Russian Five")", suffered a [brain injury](/wiki/Traumatic_brain_injury "Traumatic brain injury") in a [limousine](/wiki/Limousine "Limousine") accident, and his career came to an abrupt end.[[100]](#cite_note-Konstantinov-100)[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-1998_Wings-113) As a result, the team dedicated the [1997–98 season](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_NHL_season "1997–98 NHL season") to him.[[113]](#cite_note-1998_Wings-113) The Red Wings won the Stanley Cup in four games, this time over the [Washington Capitals](/wiki/Washington_Capitals "Washington Capitals"), and Konstantinov was brought onto the ice in his wheelchair so he could touch it.[[113]](#cite_note-1998_Wings-113)

#### Superstar acquisitions and more success (1999–2006)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Red_Wings&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Superstar acquisitions and more success (1999–2006)")]

The following season, the Red Wings appeared to be poised to win a third consecutive Stanley Cup when they acquired three-time [Norris Trophy](/wiki/Norris_Trophy "Norris Trophy") winner [Chris Chelios](/wiki/Chris_Chelios "Chris Chelios") from his hometown Chicago Blackhawks in March 1999.[[114]](#cite_note-114)[[115]](#cite_note-1999_trades-115) Also acquired at the trade deadline were defenseman [Ulf Samuelsson](/wiki/Ulf_Samuelsson "Ulf Samuelsson"), winger [Wendel Clark](/wiki/Wendel_Clark "Wendel Clark"), and goaltender [Bill Ranford](/wiki/Bill_Ranford "Bill Ranford").[[115]](#cite_note-1999_trades-115) Despite high aspirations, however, Detroit would end up losing in the conference semifinals to Colorado in six games.[[116]](#cite_note-116) In [2000](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_NHL_season "1999–2000 NHL season"), the Red Wings would finish second in the Central Division. Just like the previous season, however, they would lose to the Avalanche in the Western Conference semifinals.[[117]](#cite_note-117)[[118]](#cite_note-118)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Stanley_Cup_Red_Wings_and_Bush.jpg/250px-Stanley_Cup_Red_Wings_and_Bush.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stanley_Cup_Red_Wings_and_Bush.jpg)

The Red Wings were invited to the White House in November 2002, after winning the [Stanley Cup](/wiki/2002_Stanley_Cup_Final "2002 Stanley Cup Final").

In [2001](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_NHL_season "2000–01 NHL season"), Detroit, the NHL's second-best team in the regular season, were upset in the playoffs by the [Los Angeles Kings](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Kings "Los Angeles Kings").[[119]](#cite_note-119)[[120]](#cite_note-120) During the ensuing off-season, the team acquired goaltender [Dominik Hasek](/wiki/Dominik_Ha%C5%A1ek "Dominik Hašek") (the defending [Vezina Trophy](/wiki/Vezina_Trophy "Vezina Trophy") winner) and forwards [Luc Robitaille](/wiki/Luc_Robitaille "Luc Robitaille") and [Brett Hull](/wiki/Brett_Hull "Brett Hull").[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[122]](#cite_note-122)[[123]](#cite_note-123) Russian prospect [Pavel Datsyuk](/wiki/Pavel_Datsyuk "Pavel Datsyuk") also joined the team.[[124]](#cite_note-124) Strengthened by the additions, the Red Wings posted the league's best record in the [2001–02 regular season](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_NHL_season "2001–02 NHL season") and defeated Colorado in seven games in the conference finals after beating the [Vancouver Canucks](/wiki/Vancouver_Canucks "Vancouver Canucks") and St. Louis Blues in rounds one and two.[[125]](#cite_note-2002_Wings-125) The Red Wings then went on to capture another Stanley Cup, in five games, over the [Carolina Hurricanes](/wiki/Carolina_Hurricanes "Carolina Hurricanes"), with Nicklas Lidstrom winning the Conn Smythe Trophy as the playoffs' MVP.[[125]](#cite_note-2002_Wings-125) Bowman and Hasek both retired after the season.[[126]](#cite_note-126)[[127]](#cite_note-127)

The 2002 off-season saw the Red Wings promote associate coach [Dave Lewis](/wiki/Dave_Lewis_(ice_hockey) "Dave Lewis (ice hockey)") to the head coach position after Bowman's retirement.[[128]](#cite_note-128) In the market for a new starting goaltender after Hasek's retirement, they signed [Curtis Joseph](/wiki/Curtis_Joseph "Curtis Joseph") from the Toronto Maple Leafs to a three-year, $24 million deal.[[129]](#cite_note-129) Also new to the lineup was highly touted Swedish prospect [Henrik Zetterberg](/wiki/Henrik_Zetterberg "Henrik Zetterberg").[[130]](#cite_note-130) The Red Wings finished the season second in the Western Conference, which pitted them in the [2003 playoffs](/wiki/2003_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2003 Stanley Cup playoffs") against the seventh-seeded Mighty Ducks of Anaheim.[[131]](#cite_note-131)[[132]](#cite_note-132)[[133]](#cite_note-Ducks-133) The Mighty Ducks shocked the ice hockey world when they swept the Red Wings in four games en route to a [Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/2003_Stanley_Cup_Final "2003 Stanley Cup Final") appearance.[[134]](#cite_note-134)[[135]](#cite_note-135)

In the off-season, long time Red Wing Fedorov signed with the Mighty Ducks as a free agent.[[136]](#cite_note-136) Additionally, Hasek opted to come out of retirement and join the Red Wings for the [2003–04 season](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_NHL_season "2003–04 NHL season").[[137]](#cite_note-137) Joseph, despite being one of the highest-paid players in the NHL, spent part of the season in the minor leagues.[[138]](#cite_note-138) Hasek himself would be sidelined with a groin injury.[[139]](#cite_note-Hasek-139) Notwithstanding, the Red Wings would finish atop of the Central Division and the NHL standings.[[140]](#cite_note-140)[[141]](#cite_note-141) The Red Wings eliminated the [Nashville Predators](/wiki/Nashville_Predators "Nashville Predators") in six games in the first round of the [2004 playoffs](/wiki/2004_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2004 Stanley Cup playoffs"), which led to a second round match-up with the [Calgary Flames](/wiki/Calgary_Flames "Calgary Flames").[[142]](#cite_note-142) The teams split the first four games, and headed to Detroit for a pivotal game 5, which the Red Wings lost 1–0.[[143]](#cite_note-143) They were then eliminated two nights later in Calgary by the same score in overtime.[[144]](#cite_note-144) The Red Wings did not play in the [2004–05 season](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_NHL_season "2004–05 NHL season") due to the [lockout](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_NHL_lockout "2004–05 NHL lockout"), which canceled the entire NHL season.[[145]](#cite_note-145)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Red_Wings_vs_Blue_Jackets.jpg/250px-Red_Wings_vs_Blue_Jackets.jpg)](/wiki/File:Red_Wings_vs_Blue_Jackets.jpg)

The Red Wings during a game in the [2005–06 season](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_NHL_season "2005–06 NHL season"). They won that season's [Presidents' Trophy](/wiki/Presidents%27_Trophy "Presidents' Trophy").

On July 15, 2005, [Mike Babcock](/wiki/Mike_Babcock "Mike Babcock"), former head coach in Anaheim, became the new head coach for the Red Wings.[[146]](#cite_note-146) On November 21, 2005, defenseman [Jiri Fischer](/wiki/Ji%C5%99%C3%AD_Fischer "Jiří Fischer") went into [cardiac arrest](/wiki/Cardiac_arrest "Cardiac arrest") and collapsed on the bench during a game against the Nashville Predators.[[147]](#cite_note-147) The game was canceled because of his injury and was made up on January 23, 2006.[[148]](#cite_note-make_up-148) This was the first time in NHL history a game had been postponed due to an injury.[[149]](#cite_note-149) While the game was played for the full 60 minutes, the Predators were allowed to maintain their 1–0 lead from the original game and won 3–2.[[148]](#cite_note-make_up-148) The Red Wings won the [Presidents' Trophy](/wiki/Presidents%27_Trophy "Presidents' Trophy") with a 58–16–8 record, earning them 124 points and secured home ice advantage for the entire playoffs.[[150]](#cite_note-150)[[151]](#cite_note-151) They opened the [2006 playoffs](/wiki/2006_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2006 Stanley Cup playoffs") against the Edmonton Oilers with a 3–2 overtime victory at Joe Louis Arena, but the Oilers won four of the next five games to take the series.[[152]](#cite_note-152)[[153]](#cite_note-153)

Continuing the shakeup of the Red Wings roster, the off-season saw the departure of Brendan Shanahan, the return of Dominik Hasek and the retirement of Steve Yzerman.[[139]](#cite_note-Hasek-139)[[154]](#cite_note-154)[[155]](#cite_note-155) Yzerman retired with the distinction of having been the longest-serving team captain in NHL history.[[156]](#cite_note-156)

### The "Euro-Twins" era (2006–2017)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Red_Wings&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: The \"Euro-Twins\" era (2006–2017)")]

The Red Wings opened the [2006–07 season](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_NHL_season "2006–07 NHL season") with Nicklas Lidstrom as the new captain.[[157]](#cite_note-157) The team retired Yzerman's jersey number 19 on January 2, 2007.[[158]](#cite_note-Yzerman-158) The Red Wings finished first in the Western Conference and tied for first in the NHL with the [Buffalo Sabres](/wiki/Buffalo_Sabres "Buffalo Sabres"), but the Sabres were awarded the Presidents' Trophy because they had more wins.[[159]](#cite_note-159)[[160]](#cite_note-160)[[161]](#cite_note-161) Detroit advanced to the third round of the [2007 playoffs](/wiki/2007_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2007 Stanley Cup playoffs") after defeating Calgary and the [San Jose Sharks](/wiki/San_Jose_Sharks "San Jose Sharks") both in six games, coming back to win three-straight after the Sharks had a 2–1 series lead.[[162]](#cite_note-162)[[163]](#cite_note-163)[[164]](#cite_note-164) The Red Wings would then lose to the eventual Stanley Cup champion Anaheim Ducks in the conference finals in six games.[[165]](#cite_note-165)[[166]](#cite_note-166)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Red_Wings_2008_Stanley_Cup.jpg/250px-Red_Wings_2008_Stanley_Cup.jpg)](/wiki/File:Red_Wings_2008_Stanley_Cup.jpg)

The Red Wings posed with the Stanley Cup after they defeated the [Pittsburgh Penguins](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Penguins "Pittsburgh Penguins") in the [2008 Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/2008_Stanley_Cup_Final "2008 Stanley Cup Final").

To start the 2007–08 campaign, Zetterberg recorded at least a point in each of Detroit's first 17 games, setting a club record.[[167]](#cite_note-167) The Wings cruised to the playoffs, where they faced the Nashville Predators.[[168]](#cite_note-168) After goaltender Dominik Hasek played poorly in games 3 and 4 of the series, both losses, head coach Mike Babcock replaced him with Chris Osgood.[[169]](#cite_note-169) Osgood never left the net for the remainder of the playoffs, as the Red Wings came back in that series in six games.[[170]](#cite_note-170) In the second round, they swept the Colorado Avalanche in four games.[[171]](#cite_note-171) They then went on to the [Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/2008_Stanley_Cup_Final "2008 Stanley Cup Final") after defeating the Dallas Stars in six games in the conference finals.[[172]](#cite_note-172) The Red Wings won the Stanley Cup against the [Pittsburgh Penguins](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Penguins "Pittsburgh Penguins") in game 6 on June 4, 2008, by the score of 3–2. This was the Red Wings' fourth Stanley Cup in 11 years and their 11th overall in franchise history.[[173]](#cite_note-2008_Wings-173) Zetterberg scored the winning goal in the decisive game, and was also named the winner of the Conn Smythe Trophy as the most valuable player of the playoffs.[[173]](#cite_note-2008_Wings-173) Lidstrom became the first non-North American player to captain a Stanley Cup-winning team.[[174]](#cite_note-174)

On July 2, 2008, the Red Wings announced the signing of [Marian Hossa](/wiki/Mari%C3%A1n_Hossa "Marián Hossa").[[175]](#cite_note-175) On January 1, 2009, the Red Wings played the Chicago Blackhawks in the second [NHL Winter Classic](/wiki/2009_NHL_Winter_Classic "2009 NHL Winter Classic") at Chicago's [Wrigley Field](/wiki/Wrigley_Field "Wrigley Field"), defeating them 6–4.[[176]](#cite_note-176) Although they finished second in the conference to the San Jose Sharks, the Wings became the first team in NHL history to top 100 points in nine straight seasons.[[177]](#cite_note-177)[[178]](#cite_note-178) In the playoffs, the Red Wings swept the [Columbus Blue Jackets](/wiki/Columbus_Blue_Jackets "Columbus Blue Jackets"), then defeated the eighth-seeded Anaheim Ducks in a hard-fought seven-game series.[[179]](#cite_note-179)[[180]](#cite_note-180) They took on the vastly improved Chicago Blackhawks in the conference finals, winning in five games.[[181]](#cite_note-181) The Red Wings would face the Pittsburgh Penguins in the [Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/2009_Stanley_Cup_Final "2009 Stanley Cup Final") for a second consecutive year, but this series would feature a different outcome as the Penguins defeated the Red Wings in seven games.[[182]](#cite_note-182) The Red Wings became only the second NHL team to lose the Stanley Cup at home in game 7.[[183]](#cite_note-183)

The Red Wings began the [2009–10 NHL season](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_NHL_season "2009–10 NHL season") in [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), losing both games to the St. Louis Blues 4–3 and 5–3, respectively.[[184]](#cite_note-184) They were plagued by injuries throughout the season and lost the second most man games to injury, with only the last place Edmonton Oilers losing more.[[185]](#cite_note-Injuries-185)[[186]](#cite_note-186) The beginning of the season was a struggle for the Red Wings, with key players out of the lineup, including Zetterberg, [Tomas Holmstrom](/wiki/Tomas_Holmstr%C3%B6m "Tomas Holmström"), [Johan Franzen](/wiki/Johan_Franz%C3%A9n "Johan Franzén"), [Valtteri Filppula](/wiki/Valtteri_Filppula "Valtteri Filppula") and [Niklas Kronwall](/wiki/Niklas_Kronwall "Niklas Kronwall").[[185]](#cite_note-Injuries-185) After the Olympic break, Detroit posted a record of 13–3–2 and earned 28 points, the most by any team in the NHL.[[187]](#cite_note-187) This run helped them secure the fifth playoff seed in the Western Conference. This, however, was the first time the Red Wings did not have home ice advantage in a playoff series in ten seasons.[[188]](#cite_note-188)[[189]](#cite_note-189) Detroit won their first-round playoff series over the [Phoenix Coyotes](/wiki/Arizona_Coyotes "Arizona Coyotes") in seven games.[[190]](#cite_note-190) In the second round, they would be defeated by the San Jose Sharks in five games.[[191]](#cite_note-191)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Nicklas_Lidstrom_2010-01-31.JPG/250px-Nicklas_Lidstrom_2010-01-31.JPG)](/wiki/File:Nicklas_Lidstrom_2010-01-31.JPG)

[Nicklas Lidstrom](/wiki/Nicklas_Lidstr%C3%B6m "Nicklas Lidström") during the [2009–10 season](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_NHL_season "2009–10 NHL season"). Named captain in 2006, he maintained the position until his retirement in 2012.

A healthier Red Wings team finished the [2010–11 NHL season](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_NHL_season "2010–11 NHL season") with a 47–25–10 record and 104 points to win the Central Division title.[[192]](#cite_note-192) They once again faced the Phoenix Coyotes in the first round of the playoffs, this time sweeping them 4–0.[[193]](#cite_note-193) The Red Wings then went on to face the Sharks in round two. After losing the first three games of the series, the Red Wings won three consecutive games to force a game 7, becoming just the eighth team in NHL history to accomplish the feat.[[194]](#cite_note-194)[[195]](#cite_note-195) The Red Wings lost game 7 to the Sharks by a score of 3–2 and were eliminated.[[196]](#cite_note-196)

During the 2011 off-season, Red Wings defenseman [Brian Rafalski](/wiki/Brian_Rafalski "Brian Rafalski") retired.[[197]](#cite_note-197) Detroit soon signed free agent defenseman [Ian White](/wiki/Ian_White_(ice_hockey) "Ian White (ice hockey)") to take his place.[[198]](#cite_note-198) Long-time Red Wings Chris Osgood and [Kris Draper](/wiki/Kris_Draper "Kris Draper") also announced their retirement from hockey, with both soon taking positions within the club.[[199]](#cite_note-199)[[200]](#cite_note-200) Detroit signed goaltender [Ty Conklin](/wiki/Ty_Conklin "Ty Conklin") for his second stint with the team.[[201]](#cite_note-201) Tragedy struck the organization and the rest of the NHL with the [2011 Lokomotiv Yaroslavl plane crash](/wiki/2011_Lokomotiv_Yaroslavl_plane_crash "2011 Lokomotiv Yaroslavl plane crash"), which killed former Red Wings assistant coach [Brad McCrimmon](/wiki/Brad_McCrimmon "Brad McCrimmon") and defenseman [Ruslan Salei](/wiki/Ruslan_Salei "Ruslan Salei"), who had joined [the KHL team](/wiki/Lokomotiv_Yaroslavl "Lokomotiv Yaroslavl") during the summer. [Stefan Liv](/wiki/Stefan_Liv "Stefan Liv"), a former Red Wings goaltending prospect, was also among the fatalities.[[202]](#cite_note-202)[[203]](#cite_note-203) The Red Wings then added a patch to the left arm of their uniforms with the trio's initials.[[204]](#cite_note-204)

During the season, the Red Wings won an NHL-record 23 consecutive home games.[[205]](#cite_note-Home_streak-205)[[206]](#cite_note-206) The Red Wings also made the NHL playoffs, extending their streak of 21-straight playoff appearances, as the fifth seed.[[207]](#cite_note-207)[[208]](#cite_note-208) They were defeated in five games by their opening round opponent, the Nashville Predators.[[209]](#cite_note-209) On May 31, 2012, Nicklas Lidstrom retired.[[210]](#cite_note-210)

Zetterberg was named successor to Lidstrom as team captain.[[211]](#cite_note-211) On July 1, 2012, the first day of the NHL free agency period, the Red Wings signed Swiss forward [Damien Brunner](/wiki/Damien_Brunner "Damien Brunner") to a one-year, entry-level contract; forward [Jordin Tootoo](/wiki/Jordin_Tootoo "Jordin Tootoo") to a three-year, $5.7 million contract; and goaltender [Jonas Gustavsson](/wiki/Jonas_Gustavsson "Jonas Gustavsson") to a two-year, $3 million deal.[[212]](#cite_note-212)[[213]](#cite_note-213)[[214]](#cite_note-214)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Holland_Zetterberg_Babcock.jpg/250px-Holland_Zetterberg_Babcock.jpg)](/wiki/File:Holland_Zetterberg_Babcock.jpg)

[Ken Holland](/wiki/Ken_Holland "Ken Holland") and [Mike Babcock](/wiki/Mike_Babcock "Mike Babcock") named [Henrik Zetterberg](/wiki/Henrik_Zetterberg "Henrik Zetterberg") as the team captain in 2013.

The team won their final four games of the [2012–13 season](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_NHL_season "2012–13 NHL season") to earn the seventh seed of the playoffs. The Red Wings' 3–0 victory over the Dallas Stars on April 27, 2013, preserved their streak of 22 consecutive playoff appearances. As the seventh seed in the [2013 playoffs](/wiki/2013_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2013 Stanley Cup playoffs"), the Red Wings faced the second-seeded Anaheim Ducks.[[215]](#cite_note-215) They survived a fierce battle that included four overtime games, winning the series 4–3 after a 3–2 game 7 victory in Anaheim.[[216]](#cite_note-216) The next round pitted the Red Wings against the top-seeded Chicago Blackhawks. Despite jumping out to a 3–1 series lead, the Red Wings would ultimately lose to the eventual Stanley Cup champions in seven games.[[217]](#cite_note-217)[[218]](#cite_note-218)

On July 5, 2013, the Red Wings signed long time [Ottawa Senators](/wiki/Ottawa_Senators "Ottawa Senators") captain [Daniel Alfredsson](/wiki/Daniel_Alfredsson "Daniel Alfredsson") to a one-year contract and long time [Florida Panther](/wiki/Florida_Panthers "Florida Panthers") [Stephen Weiss](/wiki/Stephen_Weiss "Stephen Weiss") to a five-year contract.[[219]](#cite_note-219)[[220]](#cite_note-220) In the [2013–14 season](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_NHL_season "2013–14 NHL season"), the Red Wings moved to the [Atlantic Division](/wiki/Atlantic_Division_(NHL) "Atlantic Division (NHL)") of the [Eastern Conference](/wiki/Eastern_Conference_(NHL) "Eastern Conference (NHL)") as part of the NHL's realignment. The move to the Eastern Conference allowed them to play a majority of their games against teams in the [Eastern Time Zone](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone").[[221]](#cite_note-221)[[222]](#cite_note-222) On April 9, 2014, the Red Wings clinched their 23rd consecutive playoff appearance.[[223]](#cite_note-223) They would be eliminated in the first round by the Boston Bruins.[[224]](#cite_note-224)

On April 9, 2015, the Red Wings clinched their 24th consecutive playoff appearance, thus extending their streak.[[225]](#cite_note-225) The team was eliminated in the first round by the [Tampa Bay Lightning](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Lightning "Tampa Bay Lightning"). [Petr Mrazek](/wiki/Petr_Mr%C3%A1zek "Petr Mrázek") had earned the starting goaltender role from [Jimmy Howard](/wiki/Jimmy_Howard "Jimmy Howard"), and Kronwall was suspended for game 7 as Tampa Bay erased a 3–2 deficit to win the series.[[226]](#cite_note-226)[[227]](#cite_note-227) Mike Babcock, concluding the final year of his contract, left the Red Wings to become the new head coach of the Toronto Maple Leafs.[[228]](#cite_note-228) [Jeff Blashill](/wiki/Jeff_Blashill "Jeff Blashill"), head coach of the Red Wings' top minor league affiliate, the [Grand Rapids Griffins](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Griffins "Grand Rapids Griffins"), was named his successor on June 9.[[229]](#cite_note-229)

On April 9, 2016, despite the Red Wings losing 3–2 to the New York Rangers, the Ottawa Senators defeated the Boston Bruins 6–1 as the Red Wings narrowly made the playoffs and extended their streak to a 25th season.[[230]](#cite_note-230) They would lose in the first round to the Lightning again, this time in five games.[[231]](#cite_note-231)

During the off-season, Datsyuk decided to return to Russia.[[232]](#cite_note-232) On February 10, 2017, club owner Mike Ilitch died.[[233]](#cite_note-233) The Red Wings' playoff streak ended after 25 seasons in the 2016–17 season.[[234]](#cite_note-234) The Red Wings won their last game at Joe Louis Arena 4–1 on April 9, 2017, against the New Jersey Devils.[[235]](#cite_note-235)

### The opening of Little Caesars Arena and rebuilding (2017–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Red_Wings&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: The opening of Little Caesars Arena and rebuilding (2017–present)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Interior_of_little_Caesars_arena_panorama.jpg/250px-Interior_of_little_Caesars_arena_panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Interior_of_little_Caesars_arena_panorama.jpg)

Interior of Little Caesars Arena in September 2017. The Red Wings played their first regular season game at the arena a month later.

The Red Wings played their first regular season game at [Little Caesars Arena](/wiki/Little_Caesars_Arena "Little Caesars Arena") on October 5, 2017, winning 4–2 over the [Minnesota Wild](/wiki/Minnesota_Wild "Minnesota Wild").[[236]](#cite_note-236) The Red Wings finished the 2017–18 season with a 30–39–13 record. They missed the playoffs for the second season in a row, marking the first time since the early 1980s the team missed the playoffs in consecutive years.[[237]](#cite_note-237)[[238]](#cite_note-238) The Red Wings finished the 2018–19 season with a 32–40–10 record, missing the playoffs for a third consecutive season.[[239]](#cite_note-239)

On April 19, 2019, the Red Wings announced that [Steve Yzerman](/wiki/Steve_Yzerman "Steve Yzerman") rejoined the team as general manager and executive vice president.[[240]](#cite_note-240) On February 21, 2020, the Red Wings became the first team to be eliminated from playoff contention before the [trade deadline](/wiki/Trade_deadline "Trade deadline") since the [2003–04 Pittsburgh Penguins](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_Pittsburgh_Penguins_season "2003–04 Pittsburgh Penguins season").[[241]](#cite_note-241) On March 10, 2020, the Red Wings clinched the worst overall record in NHL for the first time since the 1985–86 season.[[242]](#cite_note-242) On March 12, the [2019–20 season](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_NHL_season "2019–20 NHL season") was suspended by the NHL due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[243]](#cite_note-243) On May 26, 2020, the NHL announced that the rest of the season was over for the seven teams that did not qualify for the 24-team Stanley Cup playoffs, which included the Red Wings.[[244]](#cite_note-244) With a record of 17–49–5, this was the first time since the 1985–86 season that the Red Wings finished with fewer than 20 wins.[[245]](#cite_note-245)[[246]](#cite_note-246) The Red Wings also became the second team since the 2004–05 NHL lockout, and the subsequent start of the [salary cap](/wiki/Salary_cap "Salary cap") era, to finish with a sub-.300 points percentage, along with the [2016–17 Colorado Avalanche](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_Colorado_Avalanche_season "2016–17 Colorado Avalanche season").[[247]](#cite_note-247)[[248]](#cite_note-Points_percentage-248) Their .275 points percentage was the worst for an NHL team since the [1999–2000 Atlanta Thrashers](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_Atlanta_Thrashers_season "1999–2000 Atlanta Thrashers season").[[248]](#cite_note-Points_percentage-248)

[Dylan Larkin](/wiki/Dylan_Larkin "Dylan Larkin") was named the Red Wings captain on January 13, 2021, who succeeded Henrik Zetterberg following his retirement in 2018.[[249]](#cite_note-249) On April 26, the Red Wings were eliminated from playoff contention for the fifth consecutive season.[[250]](#cite_note-250) They would ultimately finish with a 19–27–10 record.[[251]](#cite_note-251) The Red Wings finished the 2021–22 season at 32–40–10. They missed the playoff for the sixth consecutive season.[[252]](#cite_note-252) On April 30, 2022, Jeff Blashill was fired as head coach.[[253]](#cite_note-253) They then hired [Derek Lalonde](/wiki/Derek_Lalonde "Derek Lalonde") as their head coach on June 30, 2022.[[254]](#cite_note-254) During 2022–23 season, the Red Wings would re-sign Dylan Larkin to an eight-year contract extension.[[255]](#cite_note-255) The Red Wings finished the season at 35–37–10. They missed the playoffs for the seventh consecutive season.[[256]](#cite_note-256)[[257]](#cite_note-257) The Red Wings finished the 2023–24 season with a 41–32–9 record. However, they were eliminated from playoff contention for the eighth consecutive season via tiebreaker on April 16, 2024, after the Washington Capitals' 2–1 win over the Philadelphia Flyers.[[258]](#cite_note-258)

On December 26, 2024, after a slow start to the [2024–25 season](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_NHL_season "2024–25 NHL season"), the Red Wings fired head coach Lalonde and assistant head coach [Bob Boughner](/wiki/Bob_Boughner "Bob Boughner"). [Todd McLellan](/wiki/Todd_McLellan "Todd McLellan") was hired as their new head coach, with [Trent Yawney](/wiki/Trent_Yawney "Trent Yawney") as an assistant head coach.[[259]](#cite_note-259)[[260]](#cite_note-260) On April 12, 2025, the Red Wings were eliminated from playoff contention for the ninth consecutive season after the [Montreal Canadiens](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_Montreal_Canadiens_season "2024–25 Montreal Canadiens season") lost in overtime to the [Toronto Maple Leafs](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_Toronto_Maple_Leafs_season "2024–25 Toronto Maple Leafs season").[[261]](#cite_note-261) This extended their longest [playoff drought](/wiki/List_of_NHL_franchise_post-season_droughts "List of NHL franchise post-season droughts") in franchise history.[[262]](#cite_note-262) They finished the season with a 39–35–8 record.[[263]](#cite_note-263)

## Team information

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Red_Wings&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Team information")]

### Logo and uniforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Red_Wings&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Logo and uniforms")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Dylan_Larkin%2C_Detroit_Red_Wings_vs._Pittsburgh_Penguins%2C_Joe_Louis_Arena%2C_Detroit%2C_Michigan_%2821515288978%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Dylan_Larkin%2C_Detroit_Red_Wings_vs._Pittsburgh_Penguins%2C_Joe_Louis_Arena%2C_Detroit%2C_Michigan_%2821515288978%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dylan_Larkin,_Detroit_Red_Wings_vs._Pittsburgh_Penguins,_Joe_Louis_Arena,_Detroit,_Michigan_(21515288978)_(cropped).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Anaheim_Ducks_vs._Detroit_Red_Wings_Oct_8%2C_2010_37.JPG/250px-Anaheim_Ducks_vs._Detroit_Red_Wings_Oct_8%2C_2010_37.JPG)](/wiki/File:Anaheim_Ducks_vs._Detroit_Red_Wings_Oct_8,_2010_37.JPG)

In the preseason, the Red Wings wear straight serifed nameplates on their jerseys, as seen on [Dylan Larkin](/wiki/Dylan_Larkin "Dylan Larkin") (left). In the regular season and playoffs, they wear vertical arched letters, as seen on [Pavel Datsyuk](/wiki/Pavel_Datsyuk "Pavel Datsyuk") (right).

The Red Wings' [jerseys](/wiki/Hockey_jersey "Hockey jersey") (traditionally known in hockey as "sweaters") have been more or less the same since the 1930s – a white or red base with red or white piping. The only significant changes have been the replacement of the word *Detroit* with the "[winged wheel](/wiki/Winged_wheel "Winged wheel")" logo in 1932, and vertical arch lettering for the players' names and block letters in 1983.[[264]](#cite_note-264) The Red Wings wear the vertical arched letters in the regular season and playoffs, but use straight serifed nameplates during the preseason.[[265]](#cite_note-265)[[266]](#cite_note-266)

*[The Hockey News](/wiki/The_Hockey_News "The Hockey News")* voted the Red Wings' "winged wheel" logo the second best in the league in 2008.[[267]](#cite_note-267) The Red Wings, like all NHL teams, updated their jerseys to the new [Rbk Edge](/wiki/NHL_uniform#2007–2017:_Reebok_Edge "NHL uniform") standard for the 2007–08 NHL season.[[268]](#cite_note-268) The Red Wings kept their design as close to original as possible, the exceptions being: On the road (white) jersey, there was more red on the sleeves as the color panel began closer to the shoulder, and the letters of the [captain](/wiki/Captain_(ice_hockey) "Captain (ice hockey)") and [alternate captains](/wiki/Captain_(ice_hockey)#Alternate_captains "Captain (ice hockey)") were moved to the right shoulder.[[269]](#cite_note-269)[[270]](#cite_note-270)

When [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas") became the uniform outfitter starting with the 2017–18 season, the Red Wings kept the same basic look.[[271]](#cite_note-271)[[272]](#cite_note-272)

The Red Wings have rarely used any alternate logos or uniforms since the trend became popular in the 1990s, the sole exceptions were select games of the [1991–92 season](/wiki/1991%E2%80%9392_NHL_season#Throwback_uniforms "1991–92 NHL season") commemorating the league's 75th anniversary, and for a commemorative game on January 27, 1994, at [Chicago Stadium](/wiki/Chicago_Stadium "Chicago Stadium").[[273]](#cite_note-75th_anniversary-273)[[274]](#cite_note-commemorative-274) Those jerseys were based on the uniforms worn by the team (then the Detroit Cougars) in 1927–28.[[275]](#cite_note-275) The throwbacks are primarily white with five red horizontal stripes on the body, the broadest middle stripe bearing "Detroit" in bold letters, and three red stripes on the sleeves.[[273]](#cite_note-75th_anniversary-273)[[274]](#cite_note-commemorative-274) This jersey served as the basis for the uniforms worn by [Wayne Gretzky](/wiki/Wayne_Gretzky "Wayne Gretzky")'s team of [NHLPA](/wiki/NHL_Players%27_Association "NHL Players' Association") All-Stars, nicknamed the "99ers", for their exhibition tour in Europe during the [1994–95 NHL lockout](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_NHL_lockout "1994–95 NHL lockout").[[276]](#cite_note-276)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/DetroitCougars.svg/250px-DetroitCougars.svg.png)](/wiki/File:DetroitCougars.svg)

Stylized "D" logo used by the team during its inaugural season. The stylized "D" logo served as the basis for a "retro" alternate jerseys introduced in 2009.

The Red Wings wore alternative "retro" jerseys for the [2009 Winter Classic](/wiki/2009_NHL_Winter_Classic "2009 NHL Winter Classic") in Chicago. The jerseys were based on the uniforms worn by the Detroit Cougars during their inaugural season of 1926–27.[[277]](#cite_note-277) These jerseys were white, with a single bold red stripe on the sleeves and chest, and a uniquely styled white Old English *D* centered on the chest stripe.[[278]](#cite_note-278) These jerseys were also worn for their final 2009 regular season home game, again against the Chicago Blackhawks.[[279]](#cite_note-279) The Red Wings again used an alternate jersey mimicking throwback jerseys for the [2014 Winter Classic](/wiki/2014_NHL_Winter_Classic "2014 NHL Winter Classic") against the Toronto Maple Leafs at [Michigan Stadium](/wiki/Michigan_Stadium "Michigan Stadium").[[280]](#cite_note-280)

The Red Wings wore a specially designed one-time-only jersey for their [Stadium Series](/wiki/2016_NHL_Stadium_Series "2016 NHL Stadium Series") game in Denver against the Colorado Avalanche on February 27, 2016. The majority of this jersey was the traditional red, decorated with a thick diagonal white stripe running from the player's right shoulder across the front towards the left hip. The bottom of each sleeve featured a thick white stripe from wrist to elbow. The crest on the front of the jersey was a stylized red *D*. The words "Red Wings" were printed in all capital letters on the left side of the collar, and the phrase "EST. 1926" was printed inside the back of the collar. These jerseys featured the current Red Wings logo on the left shoulder cap. The names and numbers were printed larger than traditional NHL jerseys to increase visibility and player identification for fans watching the game at [Coors Field](/wiki/Coors_Field "Coors Field"), a stadium traditionally used for Major League Baseball.[[281]](#cite_note-281)[[282]](#cite_note-282)[[283]](#cite_note-283)

The Red Wings unveiled a uniform patch on September 27, 2016, to honor [Gordie Howe](/wiki/Gordie_Howe "Gordie Howe"), who died on June 10, 2016. The patch was a depiction of Howe's jersey number 9 and was worn by the team above the logo on the front of the jersey on the left side of the chest for all 82 regular season games during the [2016–17 season](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_NHL_season "2016–17 NHL season").[[284]](#cite_note-284)

The Red Wings wore a specially designed one-time-only jersey for the [Centennial Classic](/wiki/NHL_Centennial_Classic "NHL Centennial Classic") in Toronto against the Toronto Maple Leafs on January 1, 2017. It was a white jersey that had four stripes on the arms. Three of the stripes were red, while the fourth was silver. On the silver stripes were the years the Red Wings won the Stanley Cup. The logo and numbers were outlined in silver.[[285]](#cite_note-285)

For the 2020–21 season, the Red Wings wore special "Reverse Retro" alternate jerseys designed by Adidas. The uniform featured a white base and white sleeves inspired by the Red Wings' road uniforms of the Original Six era. Silver stripes replaced red stripes in commemoration of the team's 11 Stanley Cup championships and the 2017 Centennial Classic.[[286]](#cite_note-286) A second "Reverse Retro" uniform was unveiled in the 2022–23 season, using the 1991–92 throwback uniform based on the original Cougars' uniforms but with a red base and black stripes.[[287]](#cite_note-287)

The Red Wings' logo received significant media attention in August 2017 when it was discovered that a [white supremacist group](/wiki/White_supremacy "White supremacy") used a modified version of it, in which the wheel's spokes consisted of the occult [SS](/wiki/SS "SS") symbol [Black Sun](/wiki/Black_Sun_(occult_symbol) "Black Sun (occult symbol)"); it was the [aegis](/wiki/Aegis "Aegis") of their shields during the [Unite the Right rally](/wiki/Unite_the_Right_rally "Unite the Right rally") in [Charlottesville, Virginia](/wiki/Charlottesville,_Virginia "Charlottesville, Virginia"). As a result, the Red Wings condemned the group for the usage of the logo and threatened legal action.[[288]](#cite_note-Occult_Nazi_Co-optation_of_Hockey_Logo-288)[[289]](#cite_note-Red_Wings_condemn_use_of_logo_at_Va._rally-289)

On February 29, 2024, the Red Wings announced their first ever jersey patch advertisement with Priority, a local waste management company.[[290]](#cite_note-290)[[291]](#cite_note-291)

For the Red Wings' appearance in the [2025 Stadium Series](/wiki/2025_NHL_Stadium_Series "2025 NHL Stadium Series"), they wore white uniforms with a script "Detroit" lettering in red. The lower sleeves and socks were predominantly red, and the trademark "winged wheel" logo is on the left shoulder. The "Hockeytown" slogan was placed on the right leg. The uniforms were generally inspired by Detroit's automotive industry and hockey culture.[[292]](#cite_note-292)

On September 15, 2025, the Red Wings unveiled their commemorative red and vintage white centennial season uniform, featuring visual nods from Detroit Cougars and Falcons designs, lace-up collars, and an original chain-stitched version of the "Winged Wheel" crest.[[293]](#cite_note-293)

### Fan traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Red_Wings&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Fan traditions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Detroit_Red_Wings_vs._Vancouver_Canucks_-_November_10%2C_2016_%2831013559085%29.jpg/250px-Detroit_Red_Wings_vs._Vancouver_Canucks_-_November_10%2C_2016_%2831013559085%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Detroit_Red_Wings_vs._Vancouver_Canucks_-_November_10,_2016_(31013559085).jpg)

During the playoffs, Joe Louis Arena was adorned with a giant octopus, nicknamed [Al](/wiki/Al_the_Octopus "Al the Octopus"). This stems from a [fan tradition](/wiki/Legend_of_the_Octopus "Legend of the Octopus").

Main article: [Legend of the Octopus](/wiki/Legend_of_the_Octopus "Legend of the Octopus")

The "Legend of the Octopus" is a sports tradition during Detroit Red Wings playoff games, in which an octopus is thrown onto the ice surface for good luck.[[294]](#cite_note-294) During the playoffs, Joe Louis Arena was generally adorned with a giant octopus with red eyes, nicknamed "[Al](/wiki/Al_the_Octopus "Al the Octopus")" after former head ice manager [Al Sobotka](/wiki/Al_Sobotka "Al Sobotka").[[295]](#cite_note-295)

The [1952 playoffs](/wiki/1951%E2%80%9352_NHL_season#Playoffs "1951–52 NHL season") featured the start of the octopus throw. The owner of a local fish market, Peter Cusimano, threw one from the stands onto the ice. The eight legs are symbolic of the eight wins it took to win the Stanley Cup at the time. The Red Wings went on to sweep both of their opponents that year en route to a Stanley Cup championship. The NHL has, at various times, tried to eliminate this tradition but it continues to this day.[[296]](#cite_note-296)[[297]](#cite_note-297)

Sobotka was responsible for removing the thrown creatures from the ice. When the Red Wings played at Joe Louis Arena, he was known for swinging the tossed octopuses above his head when walking off the ice. On April 19, 2008, the NHL sent a memo to the Red Wings that forbade this; they said that violating the mandate would result in a $10,000 fine.[[298]](#cite_note-Fine-298) Instead, it was to up to the linesmen to remove the octopuses.[[299]](#cite_note-299) In an email to the *[Detroit Free Press](/wiki/Detroit_Free_Press "Detroit Free Press")*, NHL spokesman Frank Brown justified the ban because matter flew off the octopus and got on the ice when Sobotka swung it above his head.[[298]](#cite_note-Fine-298) This ban was later loosened to allow for the octopus twirling to take place at the Zamboni entrance.[[300]](#cite_note-300)

Typically during the last minute or two of games that the Red Wings are winning, especially around the end of the season and during the playoffs, fans are known to start singing along to [Journey](/wiki/Journey_(band) "Journey (band)")'s "[Don't Stop Believin'](/wiki/Don%27t_Stop_Believin%27 "Don't Stop Believin'")". The song is played over the PA system and continues until it is muted while the crowd sings the words "Born and raised in South Detroit," then the song resumes over the speakers in the arena.[[301]](#cite_note-301)[[302]](#cite_note-302)[[303]](#cite_note-303)

## Broadcasters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Red_Wings&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Broadcasters")]

See also: [List of Detroit Red Wings broadcasters](/wiki/List_of_Detroit_Red_Wings_broadcasters "List of Detroit Red Wings broadcasters")

The Red Wings' [flagship radio station](/wiki/Flagship_(radio) "Flagship (radio)") is [WXYT-FM](/wiki/WXYT-FM "WXYT-FM") 97.1. If there is a conflict with [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers") baseball or [Detroit Lions](/wiki/Detroit_Lions "Detroit Lions") football, games are carried on [WWJ](/wiki/WWJ_(AM) "WWJ (AM)") 950. There are several affiliate stations throughout [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan").[[304]](#cite_note-304)

Regional Red Wings games are currently aired by [FanDuel Sports Network Detroit](/wiki/FanDuel_Sports_Network_Detroit "FanDuel Sports Network Detroit").[[305]](#cite_note-305) It is anticipated that the network will close following the conclusion of the 2025–26 season; in February 2026, Ilitch Sports + Entertainment announced that the Red Wings will have their regional broadcasts distributed by [MLB Local Media](/wiki/MLB_Local_Media "MLB Local Media") beginning in the 2026–27 season. The agreement also includes the co-owned Detroit Tigers.[[306]](#cite_note-306) The next month, Ilitch Sports + Entertainment announced the creation of [Detroit SportsNet](/wiki/Detroit_SportsNet "Detroit SportsNet") to continue syndicating games to cable and satellite providers in the Red Wings' regional market.[[307]](#cite_note-307)

Announcers:

* [Ken Daniels](/wiki/Ken_Daniels "Ken Daniels"): Television play by play announcer.
* [Mickey Redmond](/wiki/Mickey_Redmond "Mickey Redmond"): Television color commentator (home games and select away games).
* [Chris Osgood](/wiki/Chris_Osgood "Chris Osgood"): Television color commentator (select away games that Redmond does not attend) and studio analyst (when not doing color commentating).
* [Larry Murphy](/wiki/Larry_Murphy_(ice_hockey) "Larry Murphy (ice hockey)"): Studio analyst and television color commentator (select away games that Redmond and Osgood do not attend).
* Trevor Thompson: Television pre-game and post-game host and reporter.
* Daniella Bruce: Television pre-game and post-game host and reporter.
* Logan Reever: Television pre-game and post-game host and reporter.
* Ken Kal: Radio play by play announcer.
* [Paul Woods](/wiki/Paul_Woods_(ice_hockey) "Paul Woods (ice hockey)"): Radio analyst.
* Jeff Riger: Primary radio intermission and post-game host.

### Honored broadcasters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Red_Wings&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Honored broadcasters")]

Four members of the Red Wings organization have received the [Foster Hewitt Memorial Award](/wiki/Foster_Hewitt_Memorial_Award "Foster Hewitt Memorial Award"):

* [Budd Lynch](/wiki/Budd_Lynch "Budd Lynch"): TV and radio play by play and color – 1949–1975 (awarded 1985)[[308]](#cite_note-Lynch-308)
* [Bruce Martyn](/wiki/Bruce_Martyn "Bruce Martyn"): Radio play by play – 1964–1995 (awarded 1991)[[309]](#cite_note-Hewitt_Award-309)
* [Mickey Redmond](/wiki/Mickey_Redmond "Mickey Redmond"): TV color commentary – 1979–1981, 1986–present (awarded 2011)[[310]](#cite_note-Redmond-310)
* [Dave Strader](/wiki/Dave_Strader "Dave Strader"): TV play by play – 1985–1996 (awarded 2017)[[311]](#cite_note-Strader-311)

Lynch called the first locally televised game at Olympia for the original [WWJ-TV](/wiki/WDIV-TV "WDIV-TV") in 1949.[[308]](#cite_note-Lynch-308) He remained with the organization for 63 years, serving as director of publicity from 1975 to 1982, and was the [public address](/wiki/Public_address "Public address") announcer from 1982 until his death in 2012.[[308]](#cite_note-Lynch-308) From 2008 to 2012, a second PA announcer was added to work alongside him, first John Fossen, then Erich Freiny.[[312]](#cite_note-312)[[313]](#cite_note-Freiny-313) Freiny took over as the sole PA announcer following Lynch's death.[[313]](#cite_note-Freiny-313)

### Hall of Fame

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Red_Wings&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Hall of Fame")]

The Detroit Red Wings acknowledge an affiliation with many inductees to the [Hockey Hall of Fame](/wiki/Hockey_Hall_of_Fame "Hockey Hall of Fame"), including 68 former players and 12 builders of the sport.[[316]](#cite_note-medgu-316) The 12 individuals recognized as builders by the Hall of Fame include former Red Wings executives, general managers, head coaches, and owners. In addition to players and builders, several broadcasters have been awarded the [Foster Hewitt Memorial Award](/wiki/Foster_Hewitt_Memorial_Award "Foster Hewitt Memorial Award") from the Hockey Hall of Fame. [Budd Lynch](/wiki/Budd_Lynch "Budd Lynch"), a radio play-by-play announcer, was the first Red Wings broadcaster to be awarded the Foster Hewitt Memorial Award. Lynch also served as Detroit's public address announcer from 1985 until his death in 2012. In addition to Lynch, [Bruce Martyn](/wiki/Bruce_Martyn "Bruce Martyn"), [Mickey Redmond](/wiki/Mickey_Redmond "Mickey Redmond"), and [Dave Strader](/wiki/Dave_Strader "Dave Strader") have also won the award.[[309]](#cite_note-Hewitt_Award-309)[[316]](#cite_note-medgu-316)

The Red Wings have [retired](/wiki/Retired_number "Retired number") nine numbers. The first number the Red Wings retired was No. 9 in 1972 in honor of Gordie Howe, who played right wing for the team from 1946 to 1971.[[321]](#cite_note-Howe's_number-322) Howe won both the [Art Ross Trophy](/wiki/Art_Ross_Trophy "Art Ross Trophy") and the [Hart Memorial Trophy](/wiki/Hart_Memorial_Trophy "Hart Memorial Trophy") six times each and won the Stanley Cup as a Red Wing four times.[[325]](#cite_note-327) In 2007, the Red Wings retired Steve Yzerman's No. 19.[[158]](#cite_note-Yzerman-158) During Yzerman's career, he won the Bill Masterton Memorial Trophy, the Conn Smythe Trophy, the [Frank J. Selke Trophy](/wiki/Frank_J._Selke_Trophy "Frank J. Selke Trophy"), the [Lester Patrick Trophy](/wiki/Lester_Patrick_Trophy "Lester Patrick Trophy"), and the [Ted Lindsay Award](/wiki/Ted_Lindsay_Award "Ted Lindsay Award").[[326]](#cite_note-Yzerman's_stats-328) Yzerman served as Detroit's captain for 19 seasons, an NHL record, and won the Stanley Cup three times as a player with the Red Wings.[[158]](#cite_note-Yzerman-158)[[326]](#cite_note-Yzerman's_stats-328)[[327]](#cite_note-329) The franchise retired [Red Kelly](/wiki/Red_Kelly "Red Kelly")'s No. 4 on February 1, 2019.[[318]](#cite_note-Kelly's_number-319) During Kelly's 13-year career with the Red Wings, he won four Stanley Cups, the [Lady Byng Memorial Trophy](/wiki/Lady_Byng_Memorial_Trophy "Lady Byng Memorial Trophy") three times, and the [James Norris Memorial Trophy](/wiki/James_Norris_Memorial_Trophy "James Norris Memorial Trophy") once.[[328]](#cite_note-330) The franchise's most recent retired number is [Sergei Fedorov](/wiki/Sergei_Fedorov "Sergei Fedorov")'s No. 91, with the ceremony taking place on January 12, 2026.[[324]](#cite_note-Fedorov-326) During his 13-year career with the Red Wings, Fedorov won the Frank J. Selke Trophy twice, the Hart Memorial Trophy and Ted Lindsay Award once, and the Stanley Cup three times.[[329]](#cite_note-331)

The Red Wings have also made the number 6 of [Larry Aurie](/wiki/Larry_Aurie "Larry Aurie") and the number 16 of [Vladimir Konstantinov](/wiki/Vladimir_Konstantinov "Vladimir Konstantinov") no longer available for issue. However, the numbers are not considered to be officially retired.[[330]](#cite_note-332) Although Aurie's number was retired in 1938 by James E. Norris, current team owners do not consider the number to be retired.[[331]](#cite_note-333) Konstantinov's number has not been issued to any player since he was permanently disabled in a vehicle accident after the [1997 Stanley Cup Final](/wiki/1997_Stanley_Cup_Final "1997 Stanley Cup Final").[[332]](#cite_note-334) Number 99 is also unavailable as it was retired by the league in honor of Wayne Gretzky.[[333]](#cite_note-335)

### Individual records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Red_Wings&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Individual records")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Dominik_Hasek_face.jpg/250px-Dominik_Hasek_face.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dominik_Hasek_face.jpg)

Recording six shutouts during the [2002 playoffs](/wiki/2002_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2002 Stanley Cup playoffs"), [Dominik Hasek](/wiki/Dominik_Ha%C5%A1ek "Dominik Hašek") set the franchise record for the most shutouts in a single postseason.

Main article: [List of Detroit Red Wings records](/wiki/List_of_Detroit_Red_Wings_records "List of Detroit Red Wings records")

* Most goals in a season: 65, [Steve Yzerman](/wiki/Steve_Yzerman "Steve Yzerman") ([1988–89](/wiki/1988%E2%80%9389_NHL_season "1988–89 NHL season"))[[338]](#cite_note-Records-340)
* Most assists in a season: 90, Steve Yzerman (1988–89)[[338]](#cite_note-Records-340)
* Most points in a season: 155, Steve Yzerman (1988–89)[[338]](#cite_note-Records-340)
* Most penalty minutes in a season: 398, [Bob Probert](/wiki/Bob_Probert "Bob Probert") ([1987–88](/wiki/1987%E2%80%9388_NHL_season "1987–88 NHL season"))[[338]](#cite_note-Records-340)
* Most points in a season, defenseman: 80, [Nicklas Lidstrom](/wiki/Nicklas_Lidstr%C3%B6m "Nicklas Lidström") ([2005–06](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_NHL_season "2005–06 NHL season"))[[339]](#cite_note-341)
* Most points in a season, rookie: 87, Steve Yzerman ([1983–84](/wiki/1983%E2%80%9384_NHL_season "1983–84 NHL season"))[[340]](#cite_note-342)
* Most wins in a season: 44, [Terry Sawchuk](/wiki/Terry_Sawchuk "Terry Sawchuk") ([1950–51](/wiki/1950%E2%80%9351_NHL_season "1950–51 NHL season") and [1951–52](/wiki/1951%E2%80%9352_NHL_season "1951–52 NHL season"))[[338]](#cite_note-Records-340)
* Most shutouts in a season: 12, Terry Sawchuk (1951–52, [1953–54](/wiki/1953%E2%80%9354_NHL_season "1953–54 NHL season"), and [1954–55](/wiki/1954%E2%80%9355_NHL_season "1954–55 NHL season")) and [Glenn Hall](/wiki/Glenn_Hall "Glenn Hall") ([1955–56](/wiki/1955%E2%80%9356_NHL_season "1955–56 NHL season"))[[338]](#cite_note-Records-340)
* Most shutouts in postseason: 6, [Dominik Hasek](/wiki/Dominik_Ha%C5%A1ek "Dominik Hašek") ([2002](/wiki/2002_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "2002 Stanley Cup playoffs"))[[341]](#cite_note-343)
* Longest home win streak in NHL history: 23 games (November 5, 2011, to February 19, 2012)[[205]](#cite_note-Home_streak-205)
* Most wins in a season in NHL history: 62 (1995–96)[[106]](#cite_note-1996_Wings-106)
